name: Farefare (Frafra)
notes: Farefare (Frafra, Gurenɛ/Gurene), a Gur / Mabia (Atlantic-Congo) language of the Upper East Region of Ghana and southern Burkina Faso. INPUT CONTRACT: text is assumed to be in the Latin Farefare orthography — the Latin alphabet WITHOUT ⟨c j q x⟩ and WITH ⟨ɛ ɩ ŋ ɔ ʋ⟩. KEY FEATURES: (1) NINE ORAL VOWELS /i ɪ e ɛ a ɔ o u ʊ/ in an ATR harmony system; ⟨ɩ⟩ = [ɪ], ⟨ʋ⟩ = [ʊ]. All vowels have long counterparts (written doubled). (2) NASAL VOWELS: five (ĩ ũ ɛ̃ ɔ̃ ã). Spelling is NOT uniform across the border: in Burkina Faso nasalisation is written with a tilde (and ⟨ẽ õ⟩ stand for /ɛ̃ ɔ̃/), while in Ghana it is written with a following letter ⟨n⟩; a Ghanaian-spelled nasal vowel is therefore indistinguishable from vowel + /n/ and is not resolved here. The tilde spellings are mapped. (3) CONSONANTS: 17 phonemes (19 counting the allophones [ɣ] of /ɡ/ and [ɾ] of /d/); ⟨y⟩ = /j/, ⟨r⟩ = the tap [ɾ], ⟨'⟩ = glottal stop /ʔ/. There are no labial-velar ⟨kp gb⟩ in the inventory. (4) TONE: Farefare is a TONE LANGUAGE (high and low, lexical and grammatical, with downdrift). The orthography DOES NOT MARK TONE, so tone is NOT RECOVERABLE from orthographic input and is not modelled here.
